output.title: Landlord Withholding Security Deposit? Your Step-by-Step Recovery Guide
output.meta_description: Is your landlord withholding your security deposit? Learn your tenant rights, what landlords can deduct, and how a demand letter gets your money back fast.
content_outline: Introduction: The Frustration of a Landlord Not Returning Your Deposit
Is My Landlord Required to Return My Deposit? Know Your State Deadline
Can a Landlord Keep Your Security Deposit? Legal vs. Illegal Deductions
What Can a Landlord Deduct From a Security Deposit? (And What They Can't)
My Landlord Won't Give Me My Deposit Back: First Steps to Take
Security Deposit Not Returned Within 30 Days? Time to Escalate
How to Write a Security Deposit Dispute Letter That Actually Works
What to Do When Your Landlord Doesn't Return Your Deposit: Court & Beyond
Conclusion: Recover Your Money With Urgency
key_points: Landlords must return security deposits within a strict state-mandated timeline—typically 14 to 60 days depending on jurisdiction.
Normal wear and tear cannot be deducted; only actual damage beyond ordinary use and unpaid rent are generally valid withholdings.
A formal security deposit dispute letter is often the fastest, most effective tool to force compliance before filing suit.
DepositBack auto-generates state-compliant demand letters so tenants can act immediately and recover withheld deposits without legal confusion.
output.cta: Stop waiting on your landlord. Create your state-specific security deposit demand letter with DepositBack in minutes and start your recovery today.
output.target_audience: Frustrated US renters dealing with a landlord withholding a security deposit, not returning a deposit on time, or making wrongful deductions.
